items.title: Debits and credits fundamentals
items.description: Double-entry accounting records every transaction in at least two accounts such that total debits equal total credits. A debit increases asset and expense accounts and decreases liability, equity, and income accounts; a credit does the reverse. The direction of an entry is defined by the account's normal balance side, not by whether the effect is intuitively 'good' or 'bad'. The accounting equation Assets = Liabilities + Equity must hold after every posting, which is the structural invariant that the debit-equals-credit rule enforces.
items.applicability: Applies to any system that posts financial movements to ledger accounts, including core banking, payment engines, and middleware that translates business events into accounting entries.
items.example: Customer deposits 1,000 cash: Debit Cash (asset) 1,000; Credit Customer Deposits (liability) 1,000. Total debits (1,000) equal total credits (1,000) and the equation stays balanced.
items.limitation: Sign conventions and account classifications vary by chart of accounts and accounting framework; confirm the specific ledger's normal-balance configuration rather than assuming.

items.title: Chart of accounts
items.description: The chart of accounts (CoA) is the structured list of all accounts an organization uses to classify financial transactions, typically organized into assets, liabilities, equity, income, and expenses. Each account has a unique code and a normal balance side, and accounts are often arranged hierarchically with parent/rollup and posting (leaf) levels. A well-designed CoA supports both regulatory and management reporting by encoding dimensions such as legal entity, cost centre, product, and currency.
items.applicability: Relevant whenever middleware maps business events to ledger accounts or validates that an inbound posting references a valid, active, postable account.
items.example: Account 1010 'Cash at Bank - USD' (asset, normal debit) rolls up to 1000 'Cash and Equivalents'; only leaf account 1010 is postable, while 1000 is a reporting rollup.
items.limitation: Account numbering schemes and segment structures are organization-specific; do not assume a universal numbering convention across systems.

items.title: General ledger
items.description: The general ledger (GL) is the central record of all financial transactions, organized by account, that produces the trial balance and financial statements. Subsidiary ledgers (for example accounts receivable, accounts payable, or a customer deposit sub-ledger) hold transaction-level detail and post summarized or detailed entries to the GL via control accounts. The GL is the system of record for accounting balances and must always remain in balance, with the sum of all account balances reflecting the accounting equation.
items.applicability: Applies to integration points where transactional systems feed the GL and where reconciliation confirms sub-ledger totals agree with their GL control accounts.
items.example: A loans sub-ledger holds per-loan principal balances; their total must equal the GL control account 1200 'Loans Receivable'. A break between the two is a reconciliation exception requiring investigation.
items.limitation: GL design (single vs. multi-entity, real-time vs. batch posting) varies by platform; this describes general principles, not a specific product's behaviour.

items.title: Journal entries
items.description: A journal entry is the atomic accounting record that posts one or more debit lines and one or more credit lines to GL accounts, carrying a date, description, and supporting references. Entries must be balanced (total debits equal total credits) before they can post, and they are typically immutable once posted, with corrections made via reversing or adjusting entries rather than edits. Journals may be system-generated from business events or manually raised, and manual journals usually require maker-checker approval.
items.applicability: Applies to any posting engine or middleware that constructs accounting entries from upstream events and enforces balance and validity before submission to the GL.
items.example: Accrue interest expense of 250: Debit Interest Expense 250; Credit Accrued Interest Payable 250. The entry posts only because debits equal credits; a later payment reverses the accrual.
items.limitation: Reversal, adjustment, and immutability rules depend on the accounting policy and ledger configuration; confirm the platform's posting and correction conventions.

items.title: Reconciliation
items.description: Reconciliation is the control process of comparing two independent records of the same activity and explaining any differences before they are accepted. Common forms include bank/nostro reconciliation (internal ledger vs. bank statement), sub-ledger to GL reconciliation, and system-to-system reconciliation across an integration boundary. Items that do not match are 'breaks' or 'exceptions' and must be aged, investigated, and resolved; unexplained breaks are a key indicator of processing errors, timing differences, or fraud.
items.applicability: Essential wherever middleware moves money or postings between systems, and a daily proof is needed that what one system sent equals what the other received and booked.
items.example: A daily nostro reconciliation matches 1,000 outbound payments in the core to the bank statement; 998 match, 1 is an in-transit timing item, and 1 is an unexplained break flagged for investigation.
items.limitation: Matching rules, tolerance thresholds, and break-aging policies are institution-specific; this describes the general control, not a mandated procedure.

items.title: Period-close checklist
items.description: Period close is the structured process of finalizing accounting records for a reporting period (daily, monthly, quarterly, or year-end) so that balances are complete, accurate, and locked. A typical checklist covers cut-off of transactions, posting of accruals and adjustments, completion of reconciliations, clearing of suspense and exception accounts, review of the trial balance, and approval before the period is hard-closed. After close, the period is locked against new postings, and any further changes are made through controlled adjusting entries in a subsequent open period.
items.applicability: Relevant to batch schedulers and middleware that must respect open/closed period status and refuse or queue postings dated into a closed period.
items.example: Month-end checklist: (1) stop transaction cut-off, (2) post accruals, (3) reconcile all sub-ledgers to GL, (4) clear suspense to zero, (5) review trial balance, (6) obtain sign-off, (7) lock the period.
items.limitation: Close timetables, materiality thresholds, and sign-off hierarchies differ by institution and framework; this is a general template, not jurisdiction-specific or audit-prescriptive guidance.

items.title: Maker/checker/approver control
items.description: Maker-checker (also called dual control or four-eyes) is a segregation-of-duties control in which the person who creates or initiates a transaction (the maker) cannot be the same person who verifies and authorizes it (the checker or approver). Some high-risk actions add further approval tiers based on amount or sensitivity. The control reduces error and fraud by ensuring an independent review before a transaction takes effect, and it is typically enforced in software by state transitions, role separation, and an immutable audit trail recording who did what and when.
items.applicability: Applies to manual journals, standing-instruction changes, payment release, static-data edits, and any privileged action in banking middleware where unilateral execution is unacceptable.
items.example: User A enters a 50,000 manual journal (status Pending). User B, a different authorized user, reviews and approves it (status Posted). The system blocks A from approving their own entry and records both actions in the audit log.
items.limitation: Threshold tiers, role definitions, and exemptions are policy-driven and vary by institution; implement them as configuration, and treat this as a general control pattern rather than a compliance mandate.

items.title: Direct debit lifecycle
items.description: A direct debit is a pull-based payment in which a payee, authorized by a mandate from the payer, initiates collections from the payer's account. The lifecycle typically spans mandate setup and validation, pre-notification to the payer, submission of the collection through a clearing scheme, settlement on the due date, and post-settlement handling of returns, rejections, and reversals. Failed or disputed collections produce return/reject reason codes that must be processed to update the collection's status and, where applicable, the customer's arrears position.
items.applicability: Relevant to middleware that manages mandates, builds collection files, processes scheme return codes, and reconciles expected collections against actual settlement.
items.example: Mandate created and validated; pre-notification sent 5 days before; collection of 200 submitted; on due date 199 settle and 1 returns with reason 'insufficient funds', triggering a retry or arrears flag.
items.limitation: Mandate rules, notification timelines, return-code sets, and retry rights differ by scheme and jurisdiction; this is a general lifecycle, not advice on a specific scheme's rulebook.

items.title: Settlement basics
items.description: Settlement is the final transfer of funds (and, where relevant, value) that discharges an obligation between parties, distinct from clearing, which is the exchange and netting of payment instructions beforehand. Settlement may be gross (each transaction settled individually, as in RTGS systems) or net (positions netted to a single figure per cycle), and it occurs on a settlement date that may differ from the trade or initiation date. Until settlement finality is reached, exposures and unsettled positions carry credit and operational risk that systems must track.
items.applicability: Applies to payment and treasury middleware that tracks instructions from initiation through clearing to confirmed settlement and reconciles settled amounts against the ledger.
items.example: Ten payments totalling 1,000,000 are netted in a clearing cycle to a single net settlement of 250,000 across the central settlement account; the ledger reflects each gross item but the cash moves once.
items.limitation: Settlement mechanics, finality rules, and timing (T+0, T+1, T+2) depend on the scheme, asset class, and jurisdiction; treat this as general background, not operational rulebook detail.

items.title: Claims handling
items.description: A claim is a request to recover or correct a payment, often arising from disputes, unauthorized transactions, errors, or scheme-defined rights such as direct debit indemnity claims and card chargebacks. Handling typically follows a case lifecycle of intake, validation against eligibility and time limits, investigation with evidence, decision, and financial resolution via refund, reversal, or rejection. Each claim should carry a status, an audit trail, and links to the original transaction so that the financial impact is posted accurately and reconciles back to the ledger.
items.applicability: Relevant to middleware that ingests claim/dispute messages, drives case workflow, and posts the resulting financial corrections through controlled journals.
items.example: Customer disputes a 200 direct debit as unauthorized; a claim is raised, validated within the allowed window, upheld, and resolved by a 200 refund posted as a reversing entry linked to the original collection.
items.limitation: Eligibility windows, indemnity rights, and chargeback rules are scheme- and jurisdiction-specific; this describes a general workflow, not the rights or obligations under any particular rulebook or law.

items.title: Arrears and aging
items.description: Arrears are amounts that are due but unpaid past their due date, and aging is the practice of bucketing those overdue amounts by how long they have been outstanding (for example current, 1-30, 31-60, 61-90, 90+ days). Aging buckets drive collections activity, provisioning, and risk classification, and movement of a balance into deeper buckets is a leading indicator of deteriorating credit quality. Accurate arrears tracking depends on correct due-date calculation, allocation of partial payments, and reversal of arrears when a payment clears or a claim is upheld.
items.applicability: Applies to loan and collections middleware that computes overdue positions daily, assigns aging buckets, and feeds collections, provisioning, and reporting.
items.example: An aging report shows a customer with 500 in 1-30 days and 500 in 31-60 days; a 500 payment is allocated oldest-first, clearing the 31-60 bucket and leaving 500 in 1-30.
items.limitation: Bucket definitions, payment-allocation order, and provisioning rules are policy- and standard-dependent; this is a general explanation, not accounting-standard or regulatory guidance.

items.title: Loan terms: capital vs interest
items.description: In a loan, capital (principal) is the original amount borrowed and still outstanding, while interest is the charge for borrowing, usually accrued on the outstanding principal over time. A repayment is split between an interest component and a principal (capital) component; in a typical amortizing loan, early payments are interest-heavy and later payments are principal-heavy as the balance falls. Correctly separating capital and interest matters because they post to different GL accounts, are taxed and reported differently, and drive distinct balances (outstanding principal vs. accrued/earned interest).
items.applicability: Relevant to loan-servicing middleware that builds amortization schedules, accrues interest, and allocates each repayment between interest and principal before posting.
items.example: Monthly payment 1,000 on a loan accruing 300 interest that month: Credit reduces principal by 700 (capital) and clears 300 interest. Postings: Debit Cash 1,000; Credit Loan Principal 700; Credit Interest Income 300.
items.limitation: Interest methods (simple, compound, day-count conventions) and amortization formulas vary by product and jurisdiction; this is general and not a substitute for the loan's specific terms or accounting treatment.

items.title: Financial report controls and data lineage
items.description: Reliable financial reporting depends on controls that ensure reported figures are complete, accurate, and traceable from source transaction to final report. Data lineage is the documented, verifiable path showing how each reported number was derived, including source systems, transformations, aggregations, and the GL accounts involved. Strong controls include reconciliations between source and report, reproducibility (the same inputs and logic reproduce the same output), version control of report logic, and an audit trail of who changed report definitions and when.
items.applicability: Applies to reporting and data-integration middleware that aggregates ledger and sub-ledger data into management or regulatory reports and must demonstrate how each figure was produced.
items.example: A capital ratio line item is traced back through its formula to specific GL accounts and source extracts; re-running the same dated inputs reproduces the identical number, and any change to the formula is logged with maker-checker approval.
items.limitation: Specific reporting controls and disclosure requirements are framework- and regulator-dependent; this describes general control principles, not authoritative regulatory or audit requirements.

items.title: Exception handling in financial processing
items.description: Exception handling is the disciplined treatment of transactions that cannot be processed straight-through because they fail validation, reconciliation, or business rules. Robust handling routes exceptions to a controlled queue or suspense account rather than silently dropping or auto-correcting them, preserves the original message for replay, and records a clear reason, status, and owner. The guiding principle is that money is never lost or duplicated: failures must be idempotent and recoverable, suspense balances must be aged and cleared to zero, and resolution should follow maker-checker where financial impact is involved.
items.applicability: Critical for payment, posting, and reconciliation middleware that must guarantee no financial event is lost, double-posted, or quietly altered when something goes wrong.
items.example: An inbound payment references an invalid account, so it posts to a suspense account and raises an exception case; an operator investigates, reposts to the correct account under maker-checker, and the suspense balance returns to zero.
items.limitation: Suspense, replay, and idempotency policies are platform-specific; this is a general engineering and control pattern, not a prescribed procedure or compliance requirement.
